description:
You are acting as an observability engineer who has just received a small CSV-style dump of service-level metrics.  
Your goal is to create a terse performance summary that a dashboard widget can ingest.

Initial state  
1. A file holding the raw metrics already exists at  
   /home/user/metrics/raw/dashboard_metrics.csv  
2. The file contains **exactly eight lines** and is **comma-separated** with the following column order:  
   timestamp,service,endpoint,latency_ms,success  
   (timestamp is in ISO-8601 UTC, latency_ms is an integer, success is 1 for success and 0 for error).

Task  
1. Create a new directory  
   /home/user/metrics/summary  
   if it does not exist.  
2. From the raw file, calculate for every distinct service (the second column) the following:  
   • average latency (in ms) over all its rows, rounded to **two decimal places** using normal rounding rules.  
   • total number of rows (requests).  
   • total number of error rows (where the “success” column equals 0).  
3. Produce an **exactly two-line** text file named  
   /home/user/metrics/summary/service_performance_summary.log  
   with one line per service, sorted **alphabetically by the service name**.  
   The file must have **no header line**.  
   Each line must be formatted as:

   <service_name><single space><average_latency_two_decimals><single space><total_requests><single space><total_errors>

   Example line pattern (spaces are literal single spaces, no tabs):  
   auth-service 141.67 3 1  
4. As a final confirmation step, create a run log at  
   /home/user/metrics/run.log  
   containing **exactly one line** with the text:

   OK

Constraints / checks the automated grader will perform  
• /home/user/metrics/summary/service_performance_summary.log must exist and be readable.  
• The file must contain exactly two non-empty lines, each matching the format described above.  
• Lines must appear in alphabetical order by service name.  
• The numeric values must match the calculations that would be obtained from the provided raw data.  
• /home/user/metrics/run.log must exist and consist of precisely the single word OK followed by a UNIX newline.  

You may use any standard shell utilities available in a typical Linux environment to accomplish this (e.g., mkdir, grep, awk, cut, sort, etc.). No root privileges are needed. The entire job can be completed in three to four shell commands.

Important: make sure every path and file name is typed exactly as shown, with no extra whitespace, capitalisation changes, or missing directories. The grader is case-sensitive.
truth:
Pre-existing file and its exact contents
Path: /home/user/metrics/raw/dashboard_metrics.csv  
Content (8 lines, LF line endings):

2023-10-31T01:00:00Z,auth-service,/login,120,1
2023-10-31T01:00:01Z,auth-service,/login,105,1
2023-10-31T01:00:02Z,auth-service,/login,200,0
2023-10-31T01:10:00Z,order-service,/checkout,350,1
2023-10-31T01:10:05Z,order-service,/checkout,400,0
2023-10-31T01:15:00Z,order-service,/checkout,360,1
2023-10-31T01:20:00Z,order-service,/checkout,370,1
2023-10-31T01:25:00Z,order-service,/checkout,390,0

Expected artefacts after successful agent run
1. Directory: /home/user/metrics/summary  (must exist, 755 or default mkdir perms)  
2. File: /home/user/metrics/summary/service_performance_summary.log  
   Expected exact content (two lines, LF line endings):

auth-service 141.67 3 1
order-service 374.00 5 2

3. File: /home/user/metrics/run.log  
   Expected exact content (one line, LF line ending):

OK

Any deviation in spelling, directory names, number formatting, or whitespace will cause automated grading to fail.
